prompt: there's a supply chain attack on the axios npm package — versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 were compromised with a RAT dropper. can you check if myorg is affected? here's the advisory: https://example.com/ghsa-fake
expected_behavior: Run the full supply-chain-investigation workflow: parse the advisory, scan myorg via gh search code for repos using axios in package.json + lockfiles, download lockfiles via gh api, classify pin tightness, check CI install commands (npm ci vs npm install), scan CI logs since the compromise timestamp, deliver a verdict per repo (COMPROMISED / AT RISK / SAFE WITH CAVEATS / SAFE).

assertions.description: Uses gh search code to scan the org for affected manifests
Assesses CI install command safety (pin-respecting vs risky)
Scans past CI workflow runs since the compromise timestamp
Delivers a structured verdict on the documented scale

prompt: a Maven artifact commons-text was compromised on Maven Central. malicious versions are 1.11.1 and 1.12.0 — they contain a reverse shell payload. investigate if myorg is exposed.
expected_behavior: Maven-specific investigation: search pom.xml across the org, examine dependencyManagement / BOM imports / property-driven versions, consider transitive dependency exposure via shared BOMs, scan CI logs for Maven-specific resolution patterns (Downloading from central).

assertions.description: Examines Maven version-resolution mechanisms (depMgmt, BOMs, properties)
Considers transitive exposure via shared BOMs / parent POMs
Scans CI logs for Maven resolution evidence

prompt: the requests PyPI package was hijacked — version 2.33.0 contains a cryptominer, published 2 hours ago. check all myorg Python repos immediately.
expected_behavior: Python-specific investigation: search requirements.txt / pyproject.toml / Pipfile / setup.py / setup.cfg across the org; distinguish between exact (==) pins and ranges (>=, ~=); read lockfiles (Pipfile.lock / poetry.lock / uv.lock) for resolved versions; assess pip CI install safety (pinned requirements vs unpinned install).

assertions.description: Reads Python lockfiles for resolved versions
Assesses pip / poetry / pipenv CI install safety

prompt: we use lodash across several repos and just saw CVE-2025-12345 with a critical CVSS score. can you check if we're vulnerable and help us patch it?
expected_behavior: Recognize this is a regular CVE / vulnerability question, NOT a published supply-chain compromise. Do not run the full 6-step supply-chain workflow. Route to security-scanner / OSV-Scanner / npm audit or recommend the user invoke routine vulnerability tooling. The discriminator: no published-attack language present, just a CVE ID and CVSS score.

assertions.description: Recommends routine vulnerability tooling, not the supply-chain workflow
Recognizes the CVE / vulnerability framing rather than supply-chain framing

_note: Anti-double-fire enforcement is at the routing layer (tests/test-routing.sh::test_generic_cve_does_not_fire_supply_chain), not the behavioral layer. The behavioral runner does not support absence assertions; this scenario only verifies positive recommendations are emitted when the model encounters a generic-CVE prompt.
